---
title: "Installing UCLCHEM"
subtitle: "Windows · Linux · macOS"
jupyter: uclchem_school
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    code-line-numbers: true
    highlight-style: github
    slide-number: true
    scrollable: true
    chalkboard: true
    mermaid-format: svg
    incremental: true
    include-in-header:
      - text: <script src="incremental-show-first.js"></script>
      - text: <script src="auto-fragment.js"></script>
      - text: "<style>.reveal .fragment { transition: none !important; }</style>"
execute:
  eval: true
  enabled: true
  echo: true
---

## Reset Demo {visibility="hidden"}

Run before presenting to start from a clean state:

```{bash}
pip uninstall uclchem -y 2>/dev/null || echo "Clean — ready to go!"
```

---

## What is UCLCHEM?

A gas-grain astrochemical code for modelling molecular abundances.

::: {.incremental .show-first}
- Written in **Fortran** (compiled), called from **Python**
- Tracks gas-phase and grain-surface chemistry together
- Multiple physics models: static clouds, collapse, shocks
:::

::: {.callout-important}
The chemical network is **hard-coded for performance**.
UCLCHEM must be compiled locally — it cannot be shipped as a pre-built wheel.
:::

---

## How UCLCHEM Works

Two components, both installed by `pip install .`:

| Layer | Role |
|---|---|
| **Python layer** | Run models, interact with results, create chemical networks |
| **Fortran core** | ODE integrator + chemistry solver, network hard-coded for speed |

```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
%%| echo: false
flowchart LR
    A["First install <br/> `pip install`"] --> B["Installed Python package<br/>+ compiled Fortran"]
    B --> C{"Change<br/>network?"}
    C -->|yes| D["MakeRates<br/>edit reactions/species"]
    D --> E["pip install .<br/>recompile"]
    E --> B
    C -->|no| F["Science!"]
```

---

# Part 1 — System Prerequisites

---

## What You Need

| Requirement | Version | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Python** | 3.10 – 3.13 | via conda or venv |
| **gfortran** | any recent | GNU Fortran compiler |
| **git** | any | to clone the repo |
| **pip** | bundled with Python | |

Python libraries (`numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, …) are installed **automatically** — no manual `pip install` for them.

---

## Choosing a Python Version

![](figures/python-eol-250318.png){fig-align="center"}

::: {.incremental .show-first}
- UCLCHEM supports Python **3.10 – 3.13**
- **3.13** is the best choice as of now, 3.14 will be supported soon
- Avoid 3.11 since it's going end of life soon
:::

---

## Install System Prerequisites {.smaller}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Linux

```{bash}
sudo apt update
sudo apt install make git python3 python3-pip gfortran
```

### Windows

Open **PowerShell as Administrator** and install WSL (takes a good while): 

```powershell
wsl --install
```

Reboot, install **Ubuntu** from the Windows App Store, then inside Ubuntu:

```{bash}
sudo apt update
sudo apt install make git python3 python3-pip gfortran
```

Your Windows files are at `/mnt/c/`, `/mnt/d/`, etc.

### macOS

Install [Xcode](https://developer.apple.com/xcode/), open it once and then install the Xcode command-line tools (opens a dialog):

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

Install conda (e.g. via [Miniforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge) or [Miniconda](https://www.anaconda.com/docs/getting-started/miniconda/main)).

Create a conda environment and activate it:
```bash
conda create -n uclchem_school
conda activate uclchem_school
```

After that you can install gfortran and python in the environment:

```{bash}
conda install -c conda-forge gfortran python=3.13 -y
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Always use `-c conda-forge` on Apple Silicon — the default channel lags behind macOS updates.
:::

### Google Colab

You can find a Colab that explains how to install UCLCHEM in Colab at [this link](https://gist.github.com/GijsVermarien/121a3b9d0e095702af225605d761eb72).

:::

---

# Part 2 — Create Your Environment

---

## Create a Python Environment {#step-2-virtual-environment}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### conda (macOS)

```{bash}
conda activate uclchem_school
```

::: {.callout-warning}
`conda activate` must be run in a **terminal** — it cannot run in a notebook cell.
:::

### venv (Linux / WSL / macOS)

```{bash}
python3 -m venv uclchem_env
source uclchem_env/bin/activate
```

Your prompt will show `(uclchem_env)`.
Deactivate later with `deactivate`.

:::

::: {.callout-note}
Always work inside your environment. Installing into the system Python causes hard-to-debug conflicts.
:::

---

# Part 3 — Environment Prerequisites

---

## Install Jupyter

With your environment active, install Jupyter so you can run the tutorial notebooks:

::: {.panel-tabset}

### conda

```{bash}
conda install -c conda-forge jupyterlab ipykernel -y
```

### pip

```{bash}
pip install jupyterlab ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user --name uclchem_school
```

:::

Then launch:

```{bash}
jupyter lab
```

---

# Part 4 — Clone & Install UCLCHEM

---

## Get the Code

::: {.panel-tabset}

### HTTPS

```bash
git clone https://github.com/uclchem/UCLCHEM.git uclchem
```

### SSH

Requires you to set up local SSH keys, but allows you to interact with Github without passwords. [How to set up SSH key](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh).

```bash
git clone git@github.com:uclchem/UCLCHEM.git uclchem
```

:::

---

## Choose a Version

By default `git clone` gives you the **main** (stable) branch.
This branch is currently lacking behind due to all features being held back for the v4.0.0 release, 
so we will switch to a release candidate branch for the tutorial.

```{bash}
git fetch
git tag            # list available releases
```

Switch to a specific release (we will be using v4.0.0-rc3 for the tutorial):
```{bash}
git checkout v4.0.0-rc3
```

You can then inspect what the latest changes to the code are:

```{bash}
git log --oneline -5
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Stick to a v4.0.0-rc3 during the workshop!
:::

---

## Install UCLCHEM

With your environment active, from the repo root:

```{bash}
cd uclchem
pip install .
```

This compiles the Fortran and installs the Python wrapper. Takes ~1 minute.

::: {.callout-warning}
If pip fails, the most likely cause is a missing or wrong `gfortran`. Rerun the installer with the `--verbose` flag to see the full compiler error message, and check the troubleshooting guide.
:::

---

## What gets installed

Declared in `pyproject.toml` — all pulled in automatically:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `numpy` / `scipy` | Numerics |
| `pandas` | Results as data frames |
| `matplotlib` / `seaborn` | Plotting |
| `pyyaml` | Config files |
| `joblib` / `tqdm` | Grid runs & progress bars |
| `h5py` / `xarray` / h5netcdf | Handling and storing simulation data |

---

# Part 5 — Check the Installation

---

## Verify the Import

Everything from here on will be Python cells, so you can either run it via `python` `ipython` or inside a Jupyter notebook. 
```{bash}
python your_script.py
```


Then check that it is installed properly by importing and checking the version:
```{python}
import uclchem
print(uclchem.version.__version__)
```

If this runs without error — **UCLCHEM is installed**.

---

## Run a First Model

```{python}
import uclchem
print(uclchem.version.__version__)
```

then:

```{python}
params = {
    "initialDens": 1e4,
    "initialTemp": 10.0,
    "finalTime":   1e6,
    "freefall":    False,
}

cloud = uclchem.model.Cloud(
    param_dict=params,
    out_species=["CO", "H2O", "CH3OH"]
)
cloud.check_error()
```

OR 
add `if __name__ == "__main__":` to the script below and run it:
```{bash}
python scripts/run_uclchem_tests.py
python scripts/plot_uclchem_tests.py
```

---

## Inspect the Results


```{python}
cloud.get_dataframes().head()
```

---

## Check Element Conservation

```{python}
cloud.check_conservation(element_list=["H", "C", "N", "O", "S"])
```

Values below **1 %** are generally acceptable.

If conservation is poor, tighten the solver tolerances and run it again:

```{python}
params["reltol"] = 1e-6
params["abstol"] = 1e-12
```

---

## Make a plot!
Then plot it:
```{python}
fig, ax = cloud.create_abundance_plot(
    species=["H", "H2", "$H", "$H2", "H2O", "$H2O", "CO", "$CO", "$CH3OH", "CH3OH"],
    figsize=(10, 7),
)
ax = ax.set(xscale="log", ylim=(1e-15, 1), xlim=(1e3, 1e6))
```
     
---

## When to Reinstall

| Trigger | Command |
|---|---|
| `git pull` update | `pip install --force-reinstall ./` |
| Fortran source edit | `pip install ./` |
| MakeRates network change | `pip install ./` |

Development mode (Python edits instant, Fortran still needs reinstall) is available if you only change Python, but can be dangerous with Fortran editing!

```bash
pip install --no-build-isolation -e .
```

---

## Summary

::: {.incremental .show-first}
- Satisfy system prerequisites (Python, gfortran, git)
- Create and activate a Python environment (conda or venv)
- Clone the UCLCHEM repo and switch to a the branch you need
- Install UCLCHEM with `pip install .`
- Verify the installation by importing, running a model, and plotting results
:::
